## 🇰🇳 Saint Kitts and Nevis

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Hello* / *Good morning*; a handshake among friends
- Use formal address (Mr., Mrs., Miss + surname) until invited to use first names
- Show interest in the heritage of being the smallest sovereign nation in the Western Hemisphere (by area and population) — and the heritage Brimstone Hill Fortress (UNESCO)
- Take time over conversation — Kittitian and Nevisian social pace is unhurried, warm Caribbean
- Tip 10–15% in restaurants where service is good (often included)

**Avoid**
- Confusing Saint Kitts and Nevis as one island — they are two distinct islands (Saint Kitts the larger, Nevis the smaller) in a federation, with Nevis having internal autonomy
- Wearing camouflage clothing — illegal in Saint Kitts and Nevis
- Beachwear away from beach or hotel pool areas
- Bringing up the various Saint Kitts-Nevis political tensions (Nevis has held referendums on full independence) casually
- Loud or boisterous behaviour in churches and villages

### Core Value
The deep heritage of being the smallest sovereign nation in the Americas — Saint Kitts and Nevis was the first English Caribbean colony (1623, on Saint Kitts) and the first French Caribbean colony (1625, on Saint Kitts) — combined with the spectacular Brimstone Hill Fortress UNESCO site (the "Gibraltar of the West Indies"), the heritage Alexander Hamilton birthplace heritage on Nevis, and the warmth of Caribbean hospitality.

### Biggest Taboo
Treating Saint Kitts and Nevis as undifferentiated Caribbean. The country has remarkable historical-architectural heritage — Saint Kitts was the first English and the first French Caribbean colony (sometimes called the "Mother Colony of the West Indies"), the Brimstone Hill Fortress is among the Caribbean's most-significant fortifications, and Nevis was the birthplace of Alexander Hamilton (the US Founding Father).

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Stewed saltfish with spicy plantains* (heritage national dish), *goat water* (heritage goat stew), *coconut dumplings*, *johnny cakes* (heritage Caribbean fried bread), and Saint Kitts and Nevis rum (the heritage Brinley Gold Shipwreck) are central. Eat with cutlery in the British-Caribbean style. The host serves the most respected guest first.

**💬 Language Notes**
English is the official language; Kittitian and Nevisian Creole (an English-based creole) is the everyday spoken language. *Hello*, *Good morning*, *Thank you*.

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Kittitian-origin items (Brinley Gold Shipwreck rum, heritage batik textiles from the Caribelle Batik on Saint Kitts, heritage spice products) are appropriate gifts to take home.

**👔 Dress Code**
Basseterre and Charlestown business is smart Caribbean-British-influenced tailoring. Smart-casual is the dining standard. Resort-and-beachwear is heritage tourist dress.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, melodic Caribbean tone; storytelling tradition
- **35% — Language & Phrasing**: English-Creole code-switching, warm courtesies
- **25% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Caribbean bearing

### Origin & Chronicle
Saint Kitts (Liamuiga in the Carib language — "fertile land") and Nevis (Oualie — "land of beautiful waters") were inhabited by Carib peoples before European contact. Saint Kitts was the first English Caribbean colony (1623, under Sir Thomas Warner) and within two years the first French Caribbean colony (1625) — the two European powers shared Saint Kitts uneasily for decades, with the Caribs being massacred in 1626. The heritage Brimstone Hill Fortress was built by enslaved Africans during the 17th–18th centuries; Saint Kitts changed hands between French and British control before the 1783 Treaty of Paris confirmed British rule. Nevis was an important British sugar colony; Alexander Hamilton (the future US Founding Father) was born in Charlestown (Nevis) in 1755 or 1757. The 1834 emancipation, the post-emancipation sugar economy, the 1967 associated statehood, and the 1983 independence as a federation shaped the modern country. Nevis has held two referendums (1998 and after) on independence from Saint Kitts; both narrowly failed. Modern Saint Kitts and Nevis balances the heritage Brimstone Hill Fortress UNESCO heritage, the heritage Hamilton birthplace, the spectacular Mount Liamuiga volcanic landscape, the citizenship-by-investment programme (one of the world's longest-running), and the major resort-tourism economy.

## 🇦🇬 Antigua and Barbuda

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Hello* / *Good morning*; a handshake among friends
- Use formal address (Mr., Mrs., Miss + surname) until invited to use first names
- Show interest in the heritage "365 beaches" Antiguan tourism heritage and the heritage Nelson's Dockyard UNESCO heritage
- Take time over conversation — Antiguan and Barbudan social pace is unhurried, warm Caribbean
- Tip 10–15% in restaurants (often included)

**Avoid**
- Wearing camouflage clothing — illegal in Antigua and Barbuda
- Beachwear away from beach or hotel pool areas
- Bringing up the 2017 Hurricane Irma devastation of Barbuda without sensitivity
- Confusing Antigua (the larger) with Barbuda (the smaller) — distinct islands within the same nation
- Loud or boisterous behaviour in churches and traditional villages

### Core Value
The heritage of the British-Caribbean naval-architectural-historical identity — Antigua's English Harbour and Nelson's Dockyard (UNESCO) is the world's only Georgian-era working naval dockyard — combined with the heritage 365-beaches Caribbean-tourism heritage, the heritage Antiguan-cricket tradition, and the warmth of Caribbean hospitality.

### Biggest Taboo
Treating Antigua as undifferentiated Caribbean. The Nelson's Dockyard UNESCO heritage, the heritage cricket-cultural identity (Antigua produced Sir Vivian Richards and Sir Andy Roberts), and the deeply distinct Antiguan and Barbudan cultural identities deserve recognition.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Fungi and pepperpot* (heritage national dish — cornmeal-and-okra with spicy meat stew), *ducana* (heritage sweet-potato dumpling), *saltfish*, fresh seafood, and Antiguan rum (English Harbour) are central. Eat with cutlery in the British-Caribbean style.

**💬 Language Notes**
English is the official language; Antiguan and Barbudan Creole (English-based creole) is the everyday spoken language. *Hello*, *Good morning*, *Thank you*.

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Antiguan-origin items (English Harbour rum, heritage Caribbean spices, Antigua-Black-Pineapple products) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
St. John's business is smart Caribbean-British-influenced tailoring. Smart-casual is the dining standard. Resort-and-beachwear is heritage tourist dress.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, melodic Caribbean tone; cricket-and-storytelling tradition
- **35% — Language & Phrasing**: English-Creole code-switching, warm courtesies
- **25% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Caribbean bearing

### Origin & Chronicle
Antigua and Barbuda were inhabited by Arawak and later Carib peoples before European contact. Christopher Columbus sighted Antigua in 1493 (naming it after the Church of Santa María La Antigua in Seville). British colonisation began in 1632; the heritage African slave trade brought enslaved Africans whose descendants form the majority of the modern population. The heritage Nelson's Dockyard (UNESCO) is the world's only working Georgian-era naval dockyard. The 1834 emancipation, the post-emancipation sugar economy, the 1981 independence, and the long Vere Bird (1981–1994) and Lester Bird (1994–2004) governments shaped the modern country. The 2017 Hurricane Irma devastated Barbuda, requiring full evacuation. Modern Antigua and Barbuda balances the heritage Nelson's Dockyard UNESCO heritage, the heritage cricket-cultural identity (Sir Vivian Richards, Sir Andy Roberts among the heritage West Indian cricket greats), the major resort-tourism economy, and the heritage distinct Antiguan and Barbudan identities.

### The Local

**🛍 Shops**

   The institutional Antiguan-rum heritage — among the heritage Caribbean-rum origins.
   *Tags: Romantic · Business*
   > Etiquette tip: English Harbour rum is the heritage architectural-rum Antigua purchase.

2. **HERITAGE · ✦ ST. JOHN'S MARKETS** — *St. John's Public Market*

   *Tags: Family · Friendly*

## 🇩🇲 Dominica

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Bonjou* (Dominican Creole) or *Hello*; a handshake among friends
- Use formal address (Mr., Mrs., Miss + surname) until invited to use first names
- Show interest in Dominica's heritage as the "Nature Island of the Caribbean"
- Note the heritage Kalinago (Carib) Territory — the largest remaining indigenous Carib population in the Caribbean
- Tip 10–15% in restaurants where service is good

**Avoid**
- Confusing Dominica (Commonwealth, English-speaking) with the Dominican Republic (Spanish-speaking) — fundamentally distinct
- Wearing camouflage clothing — illegal in Dominica
- Bringing up the 2017 Hurricane Maria devastation (which destroyed about 90% of housing) without sensitivity
- Treating the Kalinago Territory as a tourist attraction
- Loud or boisterous behaviour in churches and villages

### Core Value
The heritage of being the "Nature Island of the Caribbean" — among the world's most-rainforest-rich Caribbean islands, with the heritage Morne Trois Pitons UNESCO landscape (with the world's second-largest boiling lake) — combined with the heritage Kalinago (Carib) population, and the heritage French-British-creole cultural mix.

### Biggest Taboo
Confusing Dominica with the Dominican Republic. Dominica is a small (72,000 people) Commonwealth English-speaking nation in the Lesser Antilles; the Dominican Republic is a larger Spanish-speaking nation on Hispaniola.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Mountain chicken* (heritage frog dish — now severely restricted), *callaloo*, *bouillon*, fresh seafood, and Dominican rum are central. Eat with cutlery in the British-Caribbean style.

**💬 Language Notes**
English is the official language; Dominican Creole (a French-based *Kwéyòl*, similar to Saint Lucian) is the everyday spoken language. *Bonjou*, *Mèsi* (Kwéyòl thank you), *Hello*, *Thank you*.

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Dominican-origin items (heritage Dominican coffee, heritage Kalinago basketry — purchased directly from the Territory, heritage spice products) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
Roseau business is smart Caribbean-British-influenced tailoring. Smart-casual is the dining standard. Resort-and-beachwear is heritage tourist dress.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, melodic Caribbean-Kwéyòl tone; storytelling tradition
- **35% — Language & Phrasing**: English-Kwéyòl code-switching, warm courtesies
- **25% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Caribbean bearing

### Origin & Chronicle
Dominica was inhabited by Kalinago (Island Carib) peoples before European contact, and was among the longest-resisting Caribbean territories — the Kalinago resisted European colonisation for centuries. Christopher Columbus sighted the island on a Sunday in 1493 (naming it for the Latin *dies Dominica*). The European colonial period saw Dominica change hands between French and British rule (similar to Saint Lucia); the heritage African slave trade and the post-emancipation period shaped the population. The 1903 establishment of the Carib (Kalinago) Territory recognised the indigenous heritage; the 1978 independence within the Commonwealth shaped the modern country. The 1979 Hurricane David, the 2017 Hurricane Maria (which destroyed about 90% of housing), and the long process of climate-resilient rebuilding shape the recent chronicle. Modern Dominica balances the heritage "Nature Island" identity, the spectacular Morne Trois Pitons UNESCO landscape, the heritage Kalinago population, the citizenship-by-investment programme, and the eco-tourism economy.

## 🇬🇩 Grenada

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Hello* / *Good morning*; a handshake among friends
- Use formal address (Mr., Mrs., Miss + surname) until invited to use first names
- Show interest in Grenada as "the Spice Isle" — among the world's heritage nutmeg origins
- Take time over conversation — Grenadian social pace is unhurried, warm Caribbean
- Tip 10–15% in restaurants (often included)

**Avoid**
- Wearing camouflage clothing — illegal in Grenada
- Beachwear away from beach or hotel pool areas
- Bringing up the 1979–1983 Maurice Bishop revolutionary period and the 1983 US-led invasion casually
- Bringing up the 2004 Hurricane Ivan devastation without sensitivity
- Loud or boisterous behaviour in churches and traditional villages

### Core Value
The heritage of being "the Spice Isle" — Grenada was among the world's heritage origins of nutmeg (one-third of world supply pre-Hurricane Ivan) and other spices — combined with the heritage Carriacou-and-Petite-Martinique smaller islands, the heritage Underwater Sculpture Park (the world's first), and the heritage Caribbean revolutionary heritage.

### Biggest Taboo
Reducing Grenada to its 1983 US-invasion headlines. The country has heritage spice cultivation (nutmeg, cinnamon, cloves, mace), the heritage Underwater Sculpture Park, the heritage Carriacou heritage with the heritage *Big Drum* / *Nation Dance* African retentions, and the spectacular Caribbean natural heritage.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Oil down* (heritage national dish — breadfruit, salted meat, callaloo, dumplings cooked in coconut milk), *callaloo*, *roti*, fresh seafood, and Grenadian rum (River Antoine, Westerhall) are central. Eat with cutlery in the British-Caribbean style.

**💬 Language Notes**
English is the official language; Grenadian Creole English is the everyday spoken language; some heritage Grenadian Creole French (*Patois*) survives in the older population. *Hello*, *Good morning*, *Thank you*.

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Grenadian-origin items (heritage nutmeg-and-spices, the heritage River Antoine rum, heritage Grenada Chocolate Company) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
St. George's business is smart Caribbean-British-influenced tailoring. Smart-casual is the dining standard. Resort-and-beachwear is heritage tourist dress.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, melodic Caribbean tone; storytelling tradition
- **35% — Language & Phrasing**: English-Creole code-switching, warm courtesies
- **25% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Caribbean bearing

### Origin & Chronicle
Grenada was inhabited by Arawak and later Carib peoples before European contact. The heritage 1654 mass-suicide of the remaining Caribs at Le Morne des Sauteurs (Leapers' Hill) when faced with French conquest is among the heritage Caribbean tragedies. French colonial rule (1649–1763, 1779–1783) gave way to final British acquisition. The heritage African slave trade and the post-emancipation spice economy (especially nutmeg, introduced in the 19th century) shaped the population. The 1974 independence under Sir Eric Gairy, the 1979 Maurice Bishop New Jewel Movement revolution, the 1983 internal coup and Bishop's assassination, and the subsequent US-led invasion ("Operation Urgent Fury") shaped the modern era. The 2004 Hurricane Ivan and 2005 Hurricane Emily caused catastrophic damage. Modern Grenada balances the heritage spice industry, the heritage Carriacou and Petite Martinique smaller-islands, the heritage Underwater Sculpture Park, and the major resort-tourism economy.

   > Etiquette tip: Grenadian nutmeg-and-spices are the heritage architectural-Spice-Isle Grenada purchase.

## 🇰🇮 Kiribati

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Mauri* (I-Kiribati hello) and a handshake
- Use formal address until invited to use first names
- Show interest in Kiribati's heritage as the only country in all four hemispheres; among the world's most-low-lying countries
- Take time over conversation — I-Kiribati social pace is unhurried, deeply communal
- Show respect for elders and traditional *unimwane* (council of elders)

**Avoid**
- Bringing up climate-change-and-sea-level-rise topics in ways that sensationalise the country's vulnerability
- Photographing women, religious ceremonies, or traditional events without permission
- Wearing beachwear in villages or in central Tarawa
- Public displays of affection
- Confusing I-Kiribati with other Pacific peoples — distinct identity

### Core Value
The deep heritage of the I-Kiribati people — among the world's most-traditional Pacific cultures, with heritage Micronesian-Polynesian cultural traditions — combined with the heritage of being one of the world's most-spread-out countries (33 islands across 3.5 million km² of ocean), and the heritage of being the world's first country to see the sun each day (Kiritimati / Christmas Island).

### Biggest Taboo
Reducing Kiribati to its climate-vulnerability headlines. The country's heritage encompasses the heritage I-Kiribati cultural traditions (the *botaki* community-meeting house, the *te kainga* extended-family system), the spectacular Kiritimati / Christmas Island heritage (the world's largest coral atoll), and the heritage WWII Battle of Tarawa heritage.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Te biringaki* (heritage breadfruit-and-coconut), *te bwabwai* (heritage giant taro), fresh fish (Kiribati has the world's most fish-dependent diet), coconut, and the heritage *te karewe* (heritage sour toddy) are central. Eat with the right hand or with cutlery. Communal eating is the heritage tradition.

**💬 Language Notes**
Gilbertese (the I-Kiribati language) is the most-widely-spoken language; English is widely used. *Mauri* (hello), *Ko rabwa* (thank you), *Taiaoka* (please).

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Kiribati-origin items (heritage I-Kiribati pandanus weaving, heritage shell crafts, heritage WWII memorabilia where appropriate) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
Tarawa business is conservative Pacific-international tailoring. Modest dress is expected — covered shoulders and knees universally. The traditional *te tibuta* (women's dress) is heritage formal.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, deep Pacific-Micronesian tone; the heritage *te boboti* community-singing
- **35% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Pacific bearing; visible respect for community elders
- **25% — Language & Phrasing**: Gilbertese-English code-switching, careful diplomatic phrasing

### Origin & Chronicle
The I-Kiribati people are descended from Austronesian Micronesian seafarers who settled the Gilbert Islands perhaps 3,000 years ago. The heritage I-Kiribati culture developed in relative isolation; British contact came in the 18th–19th centuries; the 1892 Gilbert and Ellice Islands Protectorate was established. Japanese WWII occupation of Tarawa and the catastrophic November 1943 Battle of Tarawa (one of the most-deadly Pacific amphibious battles) shaped the wartime period. The 1979 independence as Kiribati (separate from Tuvalu, the former Ellice Islands) shaped the modern country. The 1995 unilateral move of the date line (so all Kiribati islands are on the same date — making Kiritimati / Christmas Island the world's first place to see the sun each day) was a heritage decision. Modern Kiribati balances the heritage I-Kiribati cultural identity, the heritage WWII Tarawa-battlefield heritage, the spectacular Kiritimati Atoll, the existential climate-change challenge (most of the country is less than 2m above sea level), and the long-distance fishing-licensing economy.

## 🇹🇻 Tuvalu

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Talofa* (Tuvaluan hello) and a handshake; close friends embrace
- Use formal address until invited to use first names
- Show interest in Tuvalu's heritage as among the world's smallest countries (about 11,000 people, 26 km² total)
- Take time over conversation — Tuvaluan social pace is unhurried, deeply communal
- Show respect for elders and traditional *aliki* (chiefs)

**Avoid**
- Bringing up climate-change-and-sea-level-rise topics in ways that sensationalise the country's vulnerability
- Photographing religious ceremonies, traditional events, or people without permission
- Wearing beachwear in villages or central Funafuti
- Public displays of affection
- Confusing Tuvaluans with other Pacific peoples — distinct Polynesian identity

### Core Value
The deep heritage of the Tuvaluan people — among the world's most-traditional Polynesian cultures, with heritage *fatele* community-singing-and-dancing — combined with the heritage of being among the world's smallest sovereign nations (the fourth-smallest by area) and most-low-lying, and the heritage of having sold its top-level domain (.tv) to fund national operations.

### Biggest Taboo
Reducing Tuvalu to its climate-vulnerability or its .tv-domain headlines. The country's heritage encompasses the heritage Polynesian cultural traditions (the *fatele* singing-and-dancing, the heritage *maneapa* community house), the deep Tuvaluan-Christian-cultural identity, and the heritage of being among the world's smallest and most-traditional Pacific nations.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Pulaka* (heritage swamp-taro), breadfruit, fresh fish, coconut, and the heritage *toddy* are central. Eat with the right hand or with cutlery. Communal eating is the heritage tradition; the *maneapa* (community-meeting house) is the heritage gathering setting.

**💬 Language Notes**
Tuvaluan and English are both official; Tuvaluan (a Polynesian language) is the most-widely-spoken; English is widely used. *Talofa* (hello), *Fakafetai* (thank you), *Fakamolemole* (please).

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Tuvalu-origin items (heritage Tuvaluan pandanus crafts, heritage shell crafts) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
Funafuti business is conservative Pacific-international tailoring. Modest dress is expected — covered shoulders and knees universally. The traditional Tuvaluan dress is heritage formal.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, deep Polynesian tone; the heritage *fatele* community-singing
- **35% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Polynesian bearing; visible respect for elders and chiefs
- **25% — Language & Phrasing**: Tuvaluan-English code-switching, warm community-courtesies

### Origin & Chronicle
The Tuvaluan people are descended from Polynesian seafarers who settled the islands perhaps 2,000 years ago. The heritage Tuvaluan culture developed in relative isolation; British contact came in the 18th–19th centuries; the 1892 Gilbert and Ellice Islands Protectorate (administered jointly with what is now Kiribati) was established. WWII saw American forces stationed in Funafuti. The 1978 independence as Tuvalu (separate from Kiribati / the former Gilbert Islands) shaped the modern country. The 2000 sale of the .tv top-level domain rights generates substantial annual revenue (about 8% of GDP). Modern Tuvalu balances the heritage Polynesian-Tuvaluan cultural identity, the heritage *fatele*-community-singing tradition, the deep Christian-cultural identity, the existential climate-change challenge (most of the country is less than 5m above sea level), and the limited-tourism economy (Tuvalu has among the lowest annual visitor numbers of any country).

## 🇳🇷 Nauru

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Ekamawir omo* (Nauruan hello) and a handshake
- Use formal address until invited to use first names
- Show interest in Nauru's unique heritage — among the world's smallest countries (about 12,500 people, 21 km²); the world's third-smallest sovereign state
- Take time over conversation — Nauruan social pace is unhurried
- Show respect for elders and clan structures

**Avoid**
- Bringing up the phosphate-mining environmental devastation, the Australian regional-processing-centre (offshore detention) controversy, or current political-economic topics with strangers
- Photographing the central plateau (the phosphate-mined area) or detention facilities
- Wearing beachwear in villages or public areas
- Public displays of affection
- Bringing up the country's per-capita-economic decline since the 1970s phosphate boom casually

### Core Value
The unique heritage of the Nauruan people — Micronesian-Polynesian cultural traditions on the world's third-smallest sovereign state — combined with the heritage of having been (briefly, in the 1970s) one of the world's wealthiest nations per capita due to phosphate mining, before the catastrophic environmental and economic decline that followed.

### Biggest Taboo
Reducing Nauru to its phosphate-decline or detention-centre headlines. The country's heritage encompasses the heritage Nauruan cultural traditions (with deep Micronesian heritage), the heritage *Anibare Bay* and *Buada Lagoon* natural sites, and the deeply distinctive Nauruan identity.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Coconut fish*, *koraiy* (heritage Nauruan pandanus-fruit dish), grilled fresh fish, breadfruit, and Nauruan tea are central. Eat with cutlery or with the right hand. Communal eating is the heritage tradition.

**💬 Language Notes**
Nauruan and English are both official; Nauruan (a Micronesian language) is the most-widely-spoken; English is widely used in business and government. *Ekamawir omo* (hello), *Tubwa kor* (thank you).

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Nauru-origin items are limited; heritage Nauruan crafts and Nauruan postage stamps are heritage purchases.

**👔 Dress Code**
Nauru business is conservative Pacific-international tailoring. Modest dress is expected — covered shoulders and knees in public. Smart-casual is the dining standard at the limited international-grade venues.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, measured Pacific-Micronesian tone
- **35% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Pacific bearing
- **25% — Language & Phrasing**: Nauruan-English code-switching, careful diplomatic phrasing on every political-economic topic

### Origin & Chronicle
The Nauruan people are descended from Austronesian Micronesian seafarers who settled the island perhaps 3,000 years ago. The traditional 12-clan structure organised pre-colonial society. German colonisation began in 1888; Australian-British-NZ Mandate after WWI; Japanese WWII occupation (with deportation of much of the population to Truk Lagoon, where many died). The 1968 independence shaped the modern country. The 1970s–1980s phosphate-mining boom briefly made Nauru one of the world's wealthiest nations per capita; the catastrophic environmental devastation (about 80% of the island has been strip-mined to a moonscape) and the depletion of phosphate by the 2000s ended the boom. The country has since taken on offshore-detention-centre roles (controversially hosting Australian asylum-seeker detention since 2001) and citizenship-by-investment programmes. Modern Nauru balances the heritage Nauruan cultural identity, the catastrophic environmental legacy of phosphate mining, the limited remaining natural-heritage areas, and the unique sovereign-microstate political situation.

## 🇲🇭 Marshall Islands

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Iakwe* (Marshallese hello, "you are a rainbow") and a handshake
- Use formal address until invited to use first names
- Show interest in the heritage Marshallese stick-charts (the heritage Pacific-navigation-tradition) and the heritage Marshallese-Micronesian cultural identity
- Take time over conversation — Marshallese social pace is unhurried, deeply communal
- Show respect for *iroij* (chiefs) — the traditional Marshallese chiefly system continues alongside democratic government

**Avoid**
- Bringing up the US nuclear-testing legacy (1946–1958, including Bikini Atoll and the catastrophic 1954 Castle Bravo test) without sensitivity — among the world's most-significant nuclear-displacement traumas
- Photographing nuclear-test sites or restricted areas
- Wearing beachwear in villages or on Majuro
- Public displays of affection
- Confusing Marshallese with other Micronesian peoples — distinct identity

### Core Value
The deep heritage of the Marshallese people — heritage Micronesian seafarers and navigators with the heritage *meto* (stick-chart) ocean-navigation tradition — combined with the heritage of being among the most-affected Pacific peoples by US nuclear testing (1946–1958), the Bikini and Enewetak Atoll diaspora, and the spectacular coral-atoll natural heritage.

### Biggest Taboo
Treating the US nuclear-testing legacy lightly. The 1946–1958 67 nuclear tests in the Marshalls (including the catastrophic 1954 Castle Bravo hydrogen-bomb test, 1,000 times larger than Hiroshima) caused massive radioactive contamination, the displacement of the Bikini and Enewetak Atoll populations, and continuing health effects across generations.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Bwiro* (heritage breadfruit), *jaibo* (heritage Marshallese tuna preparations), *kalo bobo* (heritage taro-and-coconut), fresh seafood (especially tuna), and coconut are central. Eat with the right hand or with cutlery. Communal eating is the heritage tradition.

**💬 Language Notes**
Marshallese and English are both official; Marshallese (a Micronesian language) is the most-widely-spoken; English is widely used. *Iakwe* (hello), *Kommol tata* (thank you), *Jouj im* (please).

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Marshallese-origin items (heritage Marshallese stick-charts — the heritage Pacific-navigation craft, heritage pandanus weaving) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
Majuro business is conservative Pacific-international tailoring. Modest dress is expected — covered shoulders and knees. The traditional Marshallese *kakuwon* (woven mat) is heritage.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, melodic Pacific-Micronesian tone; storytelling and navigation-tradition cadence
- **35% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Pacific bearing; visible respect for *iroij* chiefs
- **25% — Language & Phrasing**: Marshallese-English code-switching, careful phrasing on nuclear-testing legacy

### Origin & Chronicle
The Marshallese people are descended from Austronesian Micronesian seafarers who settled the islands perhaps 2,000–3,000 years ago. The heritage Marshallese navigation-tradition (the *meto* stick-charts that mapped ocean swells) is among the world's most-sophisticated traditional-navigation systems. Spanish, German, Japanese (with substantial WWI-acquired colonial development), and US (UN Trust Territory after WWII) colonisation periods shaped the modern era. The 1946–1958 US nuclear-testing programme (67 tests, including 23 at Bikini Atoll and 43 at Enewetak Atoll) caused catastrophic displacement and contamination — the Bikini and Enewetak Atoll populations remain displaced from their home atolls. The 1986 Compact of Free Association with the United States gave Marshall Islands sovereignty while preserving close US ties; the country uses the US dollar and Marshall Islanders can live and work in the US. Modern Marshall Islands balances the heritage Marshallese-Micronesian cultural identity, the heritage navigation-tradition, the catastrophic nuclear-testing legacy, the climate-change vulnerability, and the unique US-Compact relationship.

## 🇫🇲 Federated States of Micronesia

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Kaselehlie* (Pohnpeian) / *Ran annim* (Chuukese) / *Tirow* (Yapese) / *Lenwo* (Kosraean); a handshake among friends
- Use formal address until invited to use first names
- Show deep respect for traditional chiefs and elders — Micronesian society is structured around hierarchy
- Note the heritage Yapese stone-money culture — Yap is among the world's heritage exchange-medium-cultural sites
- Take time over conversation — Micronesian social pace is unhurried, deeply communal

**Avoid**
- Photographing women, religious ceremonies, or traditional events without permission
- Wearing beachwear in villages
- Bringing up topless-women (the heritage Yapese cultural tradition that has limited applicability for foreign visitors) topics inappropriately
- Public displays of affection
- Confusing the four states (Yap, Chuuk, Pohnpei, Kosrae) — they are culturally and linguistically distinct

### Core Value
The deep heritage of four distinct Micronesian states — Yap (with the heritage stone-money tradition), Chuuk (with the spectacular WWII Truk Lagoon wreck-diving), Pohnpei (with the heritage Nan Madol UNESCO archaeological city), and Kosrae (with the heritage Lelu ruins) — combined with the heritage Caroline-Islands cultural traditions and the unique sovereign-microstate political situation.

### Biggest Taboo
Treating the four FSM states as undifferentiated. Each has distinctive heritage — Yap's stone money, Chuuk's WWII wrecks, Pohnpei's Nan Madol UNESCO heritage, and Kosrae's Lelu ruins — and a distinct language and culture.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Yam, taro, breadfruit, coconut, fresh fish*, and the heritage *sakau* (kava — the heritage Pohnpeian ceremonial drink, similar to Vanuatu kava) are central. Eat with the right hand or with cutlery. Communal eating is the heritage tradition.

**💬 Language Notes**
English is the official lingua franca; Yapese, Chuukese, Pohnpeian, and Kosraean are the four state languages; many smaller languages are spoken. *Kaselehlie* (Pohnpeian hello), *Kalahngan* (Pohnpeian thank you).

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. FSM-origin items (heritage Yapese textiles, heritage Pohnpeian sakau-related items, heritage Chuukese craft, heritage Kosraean items) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
Pohnpei and FSM business is conservative Pacific-international tailoring. Modest dress is expected — covered shoulders and knees. Yap retains some heritage traditional-dress contexts where modesty rules differ.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Pacific bearing; visible respect for chiefs and elders
- **35% — Voice Expression**: Warm, deep Pacific-Micronesian tone
- **25% — Language & Phrasing**: English-state-language code-switching, careful diplomatic phrasing

### Origin & Chronicle
The Caroline Islands have been inhabited by Austronesian Micronesian seafarers for perhaps 4,000 years; each of the four FSM states has distinctive cultural traditions. The heritage Pohnpeian Nan Madol (built 1100–1500 CE) is among the world's most-significant pre-colonial Pacific architectural sites. Spanish, German, Japanese (with substantial WWI-acquired colonial development — including major naval bases at Truk Lagoon / Chuuk), and US (UN Trust Territory after WWII) colonisation periods shaped the modern era. The catastrophic February 1944 *Operation Hailstone* US air-raid on Truk Lagoon sank a massive Japanese fleet — the wrecks today are among the world's most-significant WWII diving sites. The 1986 Compact of Free Association with the United States gave FSM sovereignty while preserving close US ties. Modern FSM balances the heritage four-state cultural identities, the spectacular Nan Madol UNESCO heritage, the Truk Lagoon WWII wreck-diving heritage, the climate-change vulnerability, and the unique US-Compact relationship.

## 🇵🇼 Palau

### Quick Briefing
*The five customs to know before arrival*

**Do**
- Greet warmly with *Alii* (Palauan hello) and a handshake
- Use formal address until invited to use first names
- Sign the heritage *Palau Pledge* on arrival — this 2017 environmental pledge (printed in your passport) commits visitors to environmental respect; among the world's most-distinctive tourism heritage
- Show interest in Palau's heritage as one of the world's most-significant marine-protection nations
- Take time over conversation — Palauan social pace is unhurried, deeply communal

**Avoid**
- Touching or stepping on coral, feeding fish, or taking marine souvenirs — the Palau Pledge is taken seriously
- Photographing the heritage *bai* (men's meeting houses) ceremonies or traditional events without permission
- Wearing beachwear in villages
- Public displays of affection
- Confusing Palauans with other Micronesians — distinct identity

### Core Value
The deep heritage of being one of the world's most-significant marine-protection nations — Palau established the world's first shark sanctuary (2009), the world's largest marine reserve at the time (2015 — covering 80% of Palau's waters), and the heritage Palau Pledge environmental commitment — combined with the heritage Palauan-Micronesian cultural identity, the spectacular Rock Islands UNESCO marine heritage, and the heritage WWII Peleliu battlefield.

### Biggest Taboo
Disrespecting the marine environment. Palau has built its national identity around marine protection — the Palau Pledge is printed in every visitor's passport and committed to with a signature on arrival. Disrespecting the coral, marine life, or the traditional environmental code (*bul*) is a serious cultural offence.

### Essential Protocols

**🍽 Table Manners**
*Demok* (heritage taro-and-coconut), *fruit bat soup* (a heritage Palauan delicacy — though increasingly rare due to conservation), grilled fresh seafood, and the heritage *kemii* (Palauan fish-paste) are central. Eat with the right hand or with cutlery. Communal eating is the heritage tradition; the *bai* (men's meeting houses) is the heritage social setting.

**💬 Language Notes**
Palauan and English are both official; Palauan (an Austronesian language) is the most-widely-spoken; English is widely used. *Alii* (hello), *Sulang* (thank you), *Adang* (please).

**🎁 Gift Protocol**
Bring quality items from your home country. Palau-origin items (heritage Palauan storyboard carvings — the heritage architectural-cultural Palauan craft, heritage shell-craft) are appropriate gifts.

**👔 Dress Code**
Koror business is conservative Pacific-international tailoring. Modest dress is expected — covered shoulders and knees. Smart-casual is the dining standard.

### Communicative Presence
- **40% — Voice Expression**: Warm, melodic Palauan tone; storytelling tradition
- **35% — Presence & Posture**: Composed, dignified Pacific bearing; visible respect for elders and traditional chiefs
- **25% — Language & Phrasing**: Palauan-English code-switching, careful diplomatic phrasing

### Origin & Chronicle
The Palauan people are descended from Austronesian seafarers who settled the islands perhaps 3,000–4,000 years ago. The heritage Palauan culture developed a distinctive matrilineal society and the heritage *bai* (men's meeting house) architectural-social tradition. Spanish, German, Japanese (with substantial WWI-acquired colonial development — Koror was the centre of Japan's South Pacific Mandate), and US (UN Trust Territory after WWII) colonisation periods shaped the modern era. The catastrophic September–November 1944 Battle of Peleliu (one of the most-deadly Pacific battles) shaped the wartime period. The 1994 independence (with a Compact of Free Association with the United States) shaped the modern country. The 2009 establishment of the world's first national shark sanctuary, the 2015 Palau National Marine Sanctuary (80% of Palau's waters fully protected), and the 2017 Palau Pledge are among the world's heritage marine-conservation moments. Modern Palau balances the heritage Palauan-Micronesian cultural identity, the spectacular Rock Islands UNESCO marine-heritage, the heritage WWII Peleliu battlefield, and the heritage marine-protection identity.
